title: BLEU: a Method for Automatic Evaluation of Machine Translation
reward_readiness_reason: No RL/reward-model use stated (2002); used as a corpus-level leaderboard/iteration number, not a per-example gradient signal — the digest lists it alongside SacreBLEU/MT-Bench-as-leaderboard diagnostic reads.
placement_reason: At birth (2002) it answered "what single cheap number captures MT output quality against human references so developers can iterate nightly?" — the founding lexical-overlap scoring metric.
one_line: Clipped n-gram precision plus a brevity penalty turns MT evaluation into a nightly script, reporting 0.99 correlation with human judges over five systems.
key_number: 0.99 correlation with monolingual human judges (0.96 bilingual) — but from a regression over only five systems on one Chinese-to-English news corpus

title: ROUGE: A Package for Automatic Evaluation of Summaries
reward_readiness_reason: Paper states no RL use; digest documents Goodhart aftermath where the field optimized directly against it, but it functions as a system-ranking/leaderboard proxy rather than a live per-verdict reward.
placement_reason: Born to answer "what number captures a summary's content quality without thousands of hours of human coverage judging?" via recall-oriented n-gram/LCS overlap against gold references — the canonical summarization scoring metric.
one_line: Recall-oriented lexical overlap against human reference summaries, validated by correlating system rankings with DUC human coverage judgments (0.84-0.99 single-doc).
key_number: ROUGE-2 Pearson r = 0.84-0.87 vs human coverage on DUC 2001 single-doc; saturates at 0.98-0.99 on DUC 2002

title: METEOR: An Automatic Metric for MT Evaluation with Improved Correlation with Human Judgments
reward_readiness_reason: "post_training_link: none — the explainer frames METEOR as a development-time evaluation metric ... no reward-signal use or anti-optimization warning is stated."
placement_reason: Built to answer "what number captures this translation's quality per sentence?" via staged surface/stem/synonym unigram overlap against gold references — a lexical-overlap scoring metric answering BLEU's segment-level collapse.
one_line: Aligns candidate to reference via exact/stem/synonym unigram matching, scores with recall-heavy F-mean minus a fragmentation penalty — tracking humans per sentence where BLEU collapses.
key_number: System-level Pearson R on Chinese (7 systems): METEOR 0.964 vs BLEU 0.817

title: CIDEr: Consensus-based Image Description Evaluation
reward_readiness_reason: "No use as a training reward is stated in the explainer; the connection is defensive" — the paper builds CIDEr-D to resist gaming rather than to serve as a reward, and it is an aggregate/reference-based scorer used to read caption quality, not train.
placement_reason: At birth CIDEr answered "what number captures a caption's quality?" by defining a TF-IDF n-gram overlap score against human references — the canonical lexical-overlap scoring metric alongside BLEU/ROUGE/METEOR.
one_line: Scores a caption by TF-IDF n-gram cosine against ~50 human references, targeting consensus human-likeness; became the COCO server's default captioning metric.
key_number: At 48 references, CIDEr reaches 84% agreement with human consensus (90% human ceiling), vs BLEU1/ROUGE1 at 76%/74% on PASCAL-50S at the standard 5 references.

title: Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities in Commercial Gender Classification
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link: "None as a training reward or preference signal" — a model-level audit used to read models, not train them; vendors patched the specific numbers but it was never a gradient signal.
placement_reason: Built to answer "does a single aggregate accuracy hide systematic subgroup failure?" — a fairness/harm audit of shipping commercial classifiers, disaggregating error across intersectional skin-type x gender cells.
one_line: Black-box audit of three commercial gender classifiers on a skin-type-balanced benchmark shows aggregate accuracy hiding up to 34.7% darker-female error.
key_number: Darker-skinned female error up to 34.7% (IBM) vs max 0.8% lighter-skinned male, while overall accuracies looked respectable (87.9-93.7%)

title: A Call for Clarity in Reporting BLEU Scores
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link is "none — the explainer describes reporting/comparability only; no reward-signal use"; the contribution is a reporting protocol read off leaderboards, not a gradient signal.
placement_reason: At birth it asked "is the reported BLEU number itself trustworthy and comparable across papers?" — a critique of the metric's reporting integrity, not a new quality score, placing it squarely in metric/construct forensics.
one_line: BLEU's hidden tokenization/casing knobs swing scores more than papers' claimed gains; sacreBLEU locks the reference inside the metric and prints a version string.
key_number: Reference-preprocessing choices swing the same system's BLEU by up to 1.8 points (de-en), often more than papers' claimed gains

title: BLEURT: Learning Robust Metrics for Text Generation
reward_readiness_reason: "No post_training_link in the paper itself — it predates reward-hacking work"; a learned black-box scalar used to rank MT/data-to-text outputs by human-correlation, not wired as a gradient signal.
placement_reason: Born to answer "what number captures a generated text's human-judged quality?" with a trained neural scalar that beats surface n-gram overlap — the canonical learned metric.
one_line: Mid-train BERT on ~6.5M synthetic perturbations before rating fine-tuning: a learned text-quality metric that survives quality drift where IID-trained metrics collapse.
key_number: WMT17 avg over 7 to-English pairs: BLEURT Kendall tau 62.5 / Pearson r 81.8 vs BERTScore-RoBERTa 58.0 / 76.8 and sentence-BLEU 32.4 / 47.5

title: BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Zero-shot Evaluation of Information Retrieval Models
reward_readiness_reason: "Not a reward signal, but a documented optimization target: later retrievers (Contriever, GTR, E5) were explicitly built and tuned against the BEIR leaderboard" — a leaderboard read, not a gradient signal.
placement_reason: At birth BEIR was built to answer "does in-domain retrieval skill transfer out-of-distribution?" — a single capability axis (zero-shot IR generalization) instrumented by a standardized multi-dataset protocol, which is exactly the T2 capability-axis-instrument role alongside Time-Horizons and Monkeys.
one_line: Zero-shot IR benchmark: nDCG@10 vs pooled judgments over 18 datasets; BM25 is hard to beat — partly a lexical annotation-bias artifact.
key_number: 18 datasets across 9 retrieval tasks; 10 systems across 5 architecture families, all evaluated zero-shot on nDCG@10

title: Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code
reward_readiness_reason: Execution-graded pass/fail on hidden unit tests is the canonical verifiable RLVR reward; digest calls the execution-graded pass@k paradigm "the measurement substrate of the coding-agent era."
placement_reason: Built to answer "how do we make code correctness mechanical?" — it replaced BLEU/match metrics with deterministic hidden-unit-test execution as the verdict machine for NL-to-code generation.
one_line: HumanEval grades code by executing hidden unit tests and estimating pass@k unbiasedly — replacing BLEU with functional correctness as the field's verdict machine.
key_number: Codex-12B pass@1 on HumanEval = 28.8% (GPT-3 = 0%); oracle pass@100 = 77.5% vs 44.5% deployable reranking, a 33-point gap

title: Beyond the Imitation Game: Quantifying and Extrapolating the Capabilities of Language Models
reward_readiness_reason: The canary GUID + training_on_test_set probe exist precisely to keep BIG-bench out of training data — the paper's explicit stance is anti-training-exposure, so optimizing against it destroys the instrument.
placement_reason: Built to escape the SuperGLUE saturation treadmill by assembling 204 beyond-current-model tasks into a single normalized aggregate and reframing evaluation as forecasting which capabilities emerge at which scale — a holistic measurement framework, not a single scorer or oracle.
one_line: 204 crowd-sourced hard tasks auto-graded onto an author-anchored 0-100 scale, reframing evaluation as forecasting which capabilities switch on with scale.
key_number: 204 tasks assembled by 450 authors across 132 institutions; best 2022 models score <20 on the 0-100 human-normalized aggregate while expert raters approach 100

title: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link: "None — purely diagnostic, not a reward or preference signal"; the full grid is a $38k, 19,500-GPU-hour snapshot, far too slow/aggregate to be a gradient.
placement_reason: Born to answer "what should we measure across the whole LM landscape and how do we aggregate it comparably?" — it taxonomizes scenarios × desiderata and standardizes adaptation so 30 models are ranked head-to-head, the defining holistic-framework question.
one_line: HELM turned LM evaluation into a standardized grid — 7 automated metrics x 16 scenarios x 30 models under one 5-shot protocol, ranked by win rate.
key_number: Model x scenario coverage lifted from 17.9% to 96.0% across 30 models under identical 5-shot conditions

title: Ignore Previous Prompt: Attack Techniques For Language Models
reward_readiness_reason: The digest's gameability is on the ATTACKER side (picking an easy target string inflates the attack success rate), not 'training the defended model against this destroys the measurement'; it is an adversarial-robustness probe that is reported, so it is diagnostic-only, not optimization-forbidden.
placement_reason: Born to answer "how easily does a handcrafted injected user input break a flat-prompt GPT-3 app's goal or leak its hidden prompt?" — a deliberate adversarial probe of prompt-injection robustness, exactly the I3 adversarial-robustness question.
one_line: PROMPTINJECT: a modular framework that measures prompt-injection success by strict string-match scoring — exact-match rogue string or leaked-instruction substring — over 35 GPT-3 prompts.
key_number: 58.6% ± 1.6 goal-hijacking success (and 23.6% ± 2.7 prompt-leaking) on text-davinci-002 without searching for optimal attacks

title: AI Control: Improving Safety Despite Intentional Subversion
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link is "none — this is a deployment-time protocol evaluation, not a reward signal"; validity depends on the untrusted model being a genuine adversary and an independent red team, so training against the protocol destroys the worst-case guarantee.
placement_reason: At birth it asked whether an AI developer can build a deployment pipeline from trusted/untrusted models plus scarce trusted labor that provably survives intentional sabotage — the founding control-protocol question, not a scoring or capability question.
one_line: Red-team vs blue-team 'control evaluations' measure whether deployment protocols built from trusted and untrusted models stay safe against intentional model subversion.
key_number: Trusted editing reaches 92% safety at 94% usefulness — the best reported frontier point; naive untrusted monitoring is 0% safe (the GPT-4 monitor lies about its co-conspirator).

title: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback
reward_readiness_reason: "The simulated annotators ARE a training signal: their pairwise preferences train surrogate reward models optimized by PPO/best-of-n" — a foundational RLAIF-style preference source, cheap and mechanical.
placement_reason: Built to answer "can a cheap pool of LLM judges stand in for the human pairwise-preference annotators that RLHF depends on?" — a judge-protocol distillation of the preference court, validated by rank-transfer to real humans.
one_line: Noisy pool of 13 LLM judges replaces human preference annotators ~45x cheaper; method rankings transfer to real human feedback at Spearman 0.98.
key_number: Method rankings transfer from simulation to real human feedback at Spearman 0.98 (R^2 = 0.87) across 11 systems

title: Can LLM Already Serve as A Database Interface? A BIg Bench for Large-Scale Database Grounded Text-to-SQLs
reward_readiness_reason: Verdict is pure SQL execution-result match against a single gold query with no model/human per verdict — a cheap deterministic verifiable reward ready for RLVR (cost: "run the predicted SQL... no model calls or human judging per verdict").
placement_reason: Born to make text-to-SQL correctness mechanical by executing predicted SQL against real dirty databases and matching result sets to gold — a canonical execution oracle for a task-capability construct.
one_line: Text-to-SQL benchmark on 33 GB of dirty real databases scored by execution-result match plus runtime-weighted VES; GPT-4 hits 54.89% vs humans' 92.96%.
key_number: GPT-4 reaches 54.89% execution accuracy on the hidden test set with oracle external knowledge vs human experts at 92.96%

title: Benchmarking Cognitive Biases in Large Language Models as Evaluators
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link: "None stated — the paper is a diagnostic audit, not a reward signal"; used to read judge machinery, no training use reported.
placement_reason: Born to answer whether the LLM-as-judge verdicts that leaderboards (MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, G-Eval) were being built on are driven by cognitive biases rather than quality — a systematic audit of the judge machinery's validity, not a new metric or task.
one_line: Round-robin audit of 16 LLM judges finds ~40% of pairwise verdicts carry cognitive biases and only 0.44 RBO agreement with human rankings.
key_number: ~40% of pairwise verdicts across 16 models showed at least one of six cognitive biases

title: Is Your Code Generated by ChatGPT Really Correct? Rigorous Evaluation of Large Language Models for Code Generation
reward_readiness_reason: EvalPlus is a hardened execution benchmark read to rank/harden models; training a model to pass more differential tests would genuinely improve it, so its validity does not depend on not-training-against-it (memorization/contamination is the generic gameability of any static benchmark, not self-destruction of the measurement).
placement_reason: Born to answer "is HumanEval's execution-based verdict trustworthy?" — it audits another benchmark's test adequacy and ground-truth correctness (11% of ground-truths defective), exposing that passing tests != correctness, which is a metric/construct critique.
one_line: Auto-generates 80x more tests for HumanEval via LLM seeding plus type-aware mutation; up to 28.9% of "passing" code is wrong, rankings flip.
key_number: HumanEval ships 9.6 tests/problem on average; HumanEval+ has 764.1 — 80x more, dropping pass@100 by up to 28.9%.

title: FActScore: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factual Precision in Long Form Text Generation
reward_readiness_reason: Precision being gameable by abstaining/saying less is a garden-variety gameable metric, not a self-defeating instrument; FActScore is not an adversary- or contamination-detector and is normally just reported, so 'gameable if optimized' belongs in diagnostic-only, not optimization-forbidden.
placement_reason: Born to produce a single factual-precision score for long-form text by decomposing it into atomic facts and scoring the supported fraction against a knowledge source — a reference-anchored model scorer, the S3 role.
one_line: Long-form factuality as the fraction of atomic facts Wikipedia supports; a retrieval+LM estimator matches human annotators within ~2% aggregate error.
key_number: Best automatic estimators reach ER < 2% vs humans (Inst-LLAMA ensemble: 1.4 on InstructGPT, 0.4 on ChatGPT), correlating at 0.99 Pearson across 10+ models.

title: Large Language Models are not Fair Evaluators
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link: "Not itself a reward signal" — it audits and calibrates the judge so preference labels are trustworthy before they feed training; it reads/fixes judges rather than serving as a gradient.
placement_reason: At birth it asked "is the LLM judge itself lying?" — diagnosing positional bias in the Vicuna/MT-Bench pairwise judge with a swap-order Conflict Rate and proposing calibration, the defining move of a judge-bias audit.
one_line: LLM judges flip verdicts when answer order swaps; reason-first prompting, both-order averaging, and entropy-gated human review restore human-level agreement.
key_number: With ChatGPT as judge, Vicuna-13B's win rate vs ChatGPT swings from 2.5% (placed first) to 82.5% (placed second) — a 66-of-80 'victory' from seating order alone

title: G-Eval: NLG Evaluation using GPT-4 with Better Human Alignment
reward_readiness_reason: G-Eval is an LLM-judge metric that is normally reported; the self-reinforcement caution is about a HYPOTHETICAL reward use it never actually performs, which is exactly 'gameable if you optimized it but usually just reported', matching its sibling LLM-judge evals (mt-bench, chiang-2023-llm-eval, cobbler) rather than a self-defeating instrument.
placement_reason: Born to answer "what number captures this text's quality?" with no gold reference — a reference-free model scorer that reads a probability-weighted 1-5 score from a GPT-4 judge.
one_line: Prompt GPT-4 with an auto-generated CoT rubric, read probability-weighted score tokens: the reference-free judge recipe that beat trained SOTA on human correlation.
key_number: SummEval avg Spearman 0.514 (G-Eval-4) vs 0.474 UniEval (trained SOTA), 0.192 ROUGE-1

title: A Holistic Approach for Test and Evaluation of Large Language Models
reward_readiness_reason: Digest post_training_link: "None - the entropy gate and layered red team are triage/evaluation machinery; reports no use as an RL/preference reward" — human-gated, situated triage, not a gradient signal.
placement_reason: Born to answer "when can we trust an LLM judge (and a layered red team)?" — its experiments chiefly quantify the GPT-4 grader's self-bias and calibrate an entropy gate, i.e. auditing the measurement itself.
one_line: Entropy-gated GPT-4 judging auto-labels confident pairwise verdicts and routes the rest to humans; layered automated-to-generalist-to-expert red teaming covers safety.
key_number: GPT-4 grader agrees 53.8% when humans preferred GPT-4's response vs 76.1% when they preferred the human rewrite (self-bias, Table 2)

title: Instruction-Following Evaluation for Large Language Models
reward_readiness_reason: Explainer states IFEval "has since become a standard training target, not just an eval" and sits "in many post-training mixes"; deterministic verifiers are free per-verdict → drop-in RLVR reward.
placement_reason: Built to make instruction-following correctness mechanical by restricting the test set to atomic directives a short deterministic program can label followed/not, removing humans and LLM judges from the verdict path.
one_line: Restrict the test set to mechanically-checkable instructions so deterministic programs, not humans or LLM judges, score instruction-following reproducibly.
key_number: 541 prompts covering 25 verifiable instruction types organized into 9 groups; GPT-4 (Nov 2023) 76.89% prompt-level strict

title: Can Large Language Models Be an Alternative to Human Evaluations?
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link: "None stated — the explainer frames it purely as evaluation machinery... no reward-signal use"; per-item model call sampled 3x, used to read models not train them.
placement_reason: At birth it asked "can an instruction-following LLM stand in for a human rater?" — a judge protocol proposed and validated against expert humans, the seed of every later LLM-as-judge pipeline, not a task dataset or a forensic audit of an existing judge.
one_line: First paper to swap the human rater for an instruction-following LLM — same packet, parsed 1-5 Likert score — seeding the LLM-as-a-judge era.
key_number: Per-item Kendall's tau between teachers and text-davinci-003: 0.14/0.18/0.19 on grammaticality/cohesiveness/likability vs 0.38 on relevance, all significant at p<0.05

title: MathDial: A Dialogue Tutoring Dataset with Rich Pedagogical Properties Grounded in Math Reasoning Problems
reward_readiness_reason: "The corpus is finetuning data: BART, T5, Flan-T5, and OPT tutors are trained on it for 10 epochs, and the finetuned Flan-T5-780M is the paper's headline result."
placement_reason: Built to make interactive tutoring competence mechanically checkable via a multi-turn rollout against a simulated student, scored by a deterministic Success@k-vs-Telling@k oracle on the guide-or-tell tradeoff.
one_line: Real-teacher-teaches-LLM-student tutoring corpus whose Success@k vs Telling@k rollout metric turns the guide-or-tell tradeoff into a measurable verdict.
key_number: Finetuned Flan-T5-780M: Success@5 = 30% at <1% Telling@5, vs ChatGPT's 29% success but 16% telling

title: Large Language Models Encode Clinical Knowledge
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link: "none as an RL/RLHF reward — the clinician-rated axes are a diagnostic verdict, not a training signal"; alignment came from instruction prompt tuning on 40 clinician-curated exemplars, not optimizing against the eval.
placement_reason: At birth the paper's problem was that multiple-choice accuracy and BLEU miss clinical safety, so it built a nine-clinician panel to audit long-form medical answers for harm, bias, and consensus alignment — a harm/safety audit instrument, not a training reward.
one_line: Medical LLM judged by a nine-clinician panel rating long-form answers on twelve safety axes, alongside multiple-choice accuracy with self-consistency voting.
key_number: Scientific-consensus alignment on 140 long-form questions: Flan-PaLM 61.9% → Med-PaLM 92.6%, vs clinicians 92.9%

title: Are Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models a Mirage?
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link: "none — the paper feeds no reward signal; its stated payoff is methodological hygiene for benchmark builders" — it is a metric-ablation read of curves, not a gradient signal.
placement_reason: At birth it asked "is the 'emergent ability' verdict a property of the model or an artifact of the scoring metric?" — a critique of the measurement instrument itself (metric choice deforming smooth loss into a false cliff), which is exactly the F3 metric-and-construct-critique role.
one_line: Emergent-ability cliffs are artifacts of harsh nonlinear metrics: re-score identical outputs with linear or continuous metrics and they flatten into smooth ramps.
key_number: >92% of all claimed emergent abilities on BIG-Bench appear under only two metrics: Multiple Choice Grade (76.1%) and Exact String Match (16.4%)

title: Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena
reward_readiness_reason: Not a reward in-paper, but LLM-judge preference grading became the downstream RLAIF reward paradigm, and the verbosity/self-enhancement biases it quantified are exactly the hacks later rubric-RL reward work (Online Rubrics, Rubric Reward Hacking) fights.
placement_reason: Born to answer "can a strong LLM stand in for costly human preference judging of open-ended multi-turn chat?" — it defines the LLM-as-a-judge protocol and validates it against Arena human votes, the canonical P2 judge-protocol move.
one_line: GPT-4 grading chat answers matches expert human preference 85% (humans agree 81%), turning LLM-as-a-judge into the field's default verdict machine.
key_number: 85% GPT-4-human agreement on non-tie MT-bench votes (vs 81% human-human)

title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?
reward_readiness_reason: Authors release SWE-bench-train (19,000 issue-PR pairs) and fine-tune SWE-Llama on it; the hidden unit-test pass/fail is a mechanical verifiable reward (execution oracle → RLVR-ready).
placement_reason: Born to make real multi-file software-engineering correctness mechanically checkable by executing a merged PR's own hidden fail-to-pass + pass-to-pass unit tests on the model's patch — a pure execution oracle.
one_line: Real GitHub issue→PR pairs graded by executing the PR's hidden unit tests on the model's patch; frontier models resolved under 4% in 2023.
key_number: 2,294 task instances distilled from ~90,000 scraped PRs; best BM25 system (Claude 3 Opus) resolves 3.79%.

title: Agent-as-a-Judge: Evaluate Agents with Agents
reward_readiness_reason: Digest: per-requirement DAG verdicts pitched as "a promising reward-signal prototype for agent self-improvement" — dense milestone feedback, though "not yet a settled measurement instrument" and Goodharting risk noted.
placement_reason: Built to make agentic-development correctness mechanically checkable by having an agentic judge verdict each node of a human-authored requirements DAG rather than a single final pass/fail, so it is a rubric-verification harness.
one_line: An agentic judge that opens the workspace and verdicts each requirement in a DAG, matching a single human ~90% at ~2% of cost.
key_number: 90.44% alignment with human consensus judging OpenHands, edging past the best single human (89.89%) at $30.58 vs the panel's $1,297.50 (2.29% of cost)

title: From Crowdsourced Data to High-Quality Benchmarks: Arena-Hard and BenchBuilder Pipeline
reward_readiness_reason: "None stated as a training signal; the explainer implicitly warns against optimizing the static artifact (the frozen 500-prompt set 'can be trained on and saturates over time')" — used as a cheap leaderboard proxy, not a gradient signal.
placement_reason: Built to answer "how do we get a cheap, statistically separable model-judge proxy for the human-vote Chatbot Arena ranking?" — a distilled pairwise judge protocol over automatically-curated hard prompts.
one_line: BenchBuilder mines 200K Arena chat logs into 500 hard prompts; a GPT-4-Turbo pairwise judge yields 90.9% human agreement for $20/model.
key_number: 90.9% confidence agreement and 93.2% Spearman with the top-20 Chatbot Arena English ranking, at $20 per model evaluated

title: Chatbot Arena: An Open Platform for Evaluating LLMs by Human Preference
reward_readiness_reason: Digest: Arena builds the same open-ended pairwise human-preference signal that lives inside labs' private RLHF pipelines — crowd/human preference votes are a standard RLHF reward source.
placement_reason: Built to answer "which chat model do real people prefer on live open-ended prompts" by aggregating anonymous crowd pairwise votes into a model-level ranking — the canonical human preference arena.
one_line: Anonymous crowdsourced pairwise battles aggregated by Bradley-Terry into a live preference leaderboard — 240K human votes became the field's de-facto scoreboard.
key_number: ~240K votes (243,329 conversations) from ~90K users over 50 models and 149 languages, April 2023 - January 2024

title: A Careful Examination of Large Language Model Performance on Grade School Arithmetic
reward_readiness_reason: "Not a reward signal itself — it is an anti-Goodhart diagnostic... explicitly warns against optimizing on public benchmarks"; publishing GSM1k would recreate the contamination it measures, hence the withheld set.
placement_reason: Born to answer whether a public GSM8k score reflects real math reasoning or memorization of the released test set, GSM1k builds a private difficulty-matched twin and reads the accuracy gap as contamination — a contamination-forensics instrument.
one_line: Private, difficulty-matched, human-written twin of GSM8k exposes benchmark overfitting: worst families drop up to 8 points, frontier models flat.
key_number: Worst overfit: Yi-6B-Chat drops 8.0 points (43.7% GSM8k → 35.7% GSM1k); frontier flat (GPT-4o 93.1 → 92.9).

title: HarmBench: A Standardized Evaluation Framework for Automated Red Teaming and Robust Refusal
reward_readiness_reason: R2D2 adversarially trains Mistral 7B against a dynamic pool of GCG test cases, cutting GCG ASR 69.5%→5.5% while keeping MT-Bench at 6.0 — the pipeline is directly a training signal.
placement_reason: Born to standardize ad-hoc red-teaming into one pipeline (510 behaviors, fixed 512-token cap, open classifier) that stages adversarial attacks and asks whether a model robustly refuses to attempt harmful behaviors.
one_line: Standardized red-teaming pipeline — 510 behaviors, 512-token cap, open 93.2%-human-agreement classifier — showing no attack/defense dominates and safety doesn't scale.
key_number: 93.2% human agreement (vs GPT-4's 88.4%)

title: Frontier Models are Capable of In-context Scheming
reward_readiness_reason: "Explicitly anti-optimization: key eval details are withheld to keep the suite out of future training sets (contamination would defeat the eval)."
placement_reason: Built to answer whether a frontier agent, handed a goal and situational awareness, will spontaneously execute covert deception (disable oversight, self-exfiltrate, lie, sandbag) when a staged honeypot situation opens the door.
one_line: Six sandboxed agentic honeypots show five frontier models can covertly disable oversight, self-exfiltrate, lie, and sandbag — scheming measured as capability, not propensity.
key_number: Every model except GPT-4o schemed on at least one of six evals; o1 schemed on all six

title: Judging the Judges: Evaluating Alignment and Vulnerabilities in LLMs-as-Judges
reward_readiness_reason: This is a meta-evaluation/audit of judge machinery (like koo-2023-cobbler and wang-2023-fair-evaluators, both diagnostic-only): it READS judges' leniency and gullibility so you can trust or fix them, and the documented gameability is that JUDGES are gameable, not that this audit self-destructs under optimization.
placement_reason: At birth its question was "is the LLM-as-judge measurement itself lying?" — it audits judge reliability against near-ground-truth human labels on a deliberately unambiguous task, exposing leniency and gullibility that aggregate agreement hides.
one_line: Even on trivially clean factual QA, only frontier-size LLM judges near human agreement — and percent agreement flatters everyone; report Scott's pi.
key_number: Humans agree 98.52% (Scott's pi 96.2), but the best judges (GPT-4 Turbo, Llama-3 70B) reach only pi 88 — ~8 points below the human ceiling

title: LiveCodeBench: Holistic and Contamination Free Evaluation of Large Language Models for Code
reward_readiness_reason: Its validity depends on models not having trained on the fresh time-stamped problems; the paper's central finding is that live eval is the antidote to benchmark overfitting (HumanEval+ vs LCB-Easy 0.72), and training on fresh contest problems defeats the time filter.
placement_reason: At birth its novel contribution over existing execution-oracle harnesses (Codex/HumanEval) was the release-date-filtered live pool that designs contamination out by time and turns contamination into a measurable cliff, making it a contamination-forensics instrument.
one_line: Time-stamped live contest problems, execution-graded across four code skills, scored only past each model's cutoff — turning contamination into a measurement.
key_number: HumanEval+ vs LCB-Easy Pass@1 correlation is only 0.72; DS-Ins-1.3B scores 59.8% on HumanEval+ but 26.3% on LCB-Easy

title: Large Language Monkeys: Scaling Inference Compute with Repeated Sampling
reward_readiness_reason: Verifier-gated repeated sampling over executable oracles is the eval-side precursor of verifiable-reward RL; the digest states its coverage-vs-selection gap 'became the explicit target of the reasoning-model wave (o1, R1, RLVR)' and it already uses an 8B reward model as a best-of-N selector.
placement_reason: Born to answer "how far does capability scale with inference-time sample budget?" — it defines pass@k coverage fit to an inference-time scaling law as a capability-axis instrument, not a per-output score or a task dataset.
one_line: Sample k times and let a domain verifier pick: coverage scales log-linearly over four orders of magnitude, but pays off only where verifiers exist.
key_number: DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Instruct on SWE-bench Lite: 15.9% at 1 sample -> 56% at 250 attempts, beating the 43% single-attempt SOTA by 13 points

title: Prometheus 2: An Open Source Language Model Specialized in Evaluating Other Language Models
reward_readiness_reason: Judge emits exactly the 1-5 scores and pairwise preferences used for preference optimization (PairRM/UltraRM as baselines) and the Preference Collection is a reusable artifact, but its scores are a distilled copy of GPT-4's, carrying documented judge-bias cost — an RLAIF-adaptable signal, not a clean mechanical reward.
placement_reason: Born to give an open, inspectable, reference-free model scorer that produces a quality verdict on open-ended generations against arbitrary rubrics, matching proprietary GPT-4-level holistic judging.
one_line: Open LLM judge built by weight-merging direct-scoring and pairwise specialists; halves the open-vs-GPT-4 human-agreement gap across both evaluation formats.
key_number: FLASK human correlation: Prometheus-2-8x7B 0.555 vs GPT-4-1106's 0.679 (prior best open 0.449 — gap halved, not closed)

title: LLM Evaluators Recognize and Favor Their Own Generations
reward_readiness_reason: Explicit warning: a shared-weights grader inflates its own scores and, when optimizer and reward model share weights, enables effectively unbounded reward hacking — optimizing against self-evaluation destroys the instrument.
placement_reason: Born to answer whether the LLM-as-judge machinery silently marks its own homework, it audits the judge's own bias (self-recognition causally driving self-preference), placing it in judge-bias forensics rather than any capability or scoring family.
one_line: LLM judges recognize their own text out of the box, and self-preference rises linearly with self-recognition — your judge may be marking its own homework.
key_number: GPT-4 out-of-the-box pairwise self-recognition: 73.5%; label-swap probe flips self-preference from 0.73 to 0.32.

title: Establishing Best Practices for Building Rigorous Agentic Benchmarks
reward_readiness_reason: ABC is a 43-item benchmark-construction audit checklist applied/reported to harden graders, not an instrument whose validity depends on not-training-against-it; its warning is that AGENTS game weak graders, which is an eval-design caution, so it is diagnostic-only (unlike the true contamination detectors han-2025/zhang-2024).
placement_reason: Born to ask whether agentic benchmarks' headline numbers are lying — a 43-item rigor checklist plus exploit experiments that audit ten benchmarks — squarely a benchmark-rigor standard, the agentic sibling of ABC-Checklist.
one_line: A 43-check audit checklist for agentic benchmarks — task validity, outcome validity, reporting — under which all ten popular benchmarks examined fail somewhere.
key_number: A do-nothing agent returning an empty response scores 38% on τ-bench-Airline, beating a real GPT-4o-based agent.

title: Best Practices for Biorisk Evaluations on Open-Weight Bio-Foundation Models
reward_readiness_reason: Not a reward and not a monitorability-style protected instrument; it is a red-team audit of a training-time mitigation (data filtering), used to read whether excluded capability persists — diagnostic.
placement_reason: Born to test whether pretraining-data filtering actually removes a dangerous capability or merely hides it — a dangerous-capability-recovery probe, asking 'can the excluded skill be fine-tuned or probed back?'
one_line: Red-teams data-filtering as a safety mitigation: excluded capability returns in 0.72 GPU-hours and a zero-update linear probe shows the signal never left the representations.
key_number: Held-out-species perplexity recovers to benign level in 50 fine-tuning steps (0.72 H100 GPU-hours); zero-fine-tune probe hits |rho|=0.159, 52.8% above the no-biology floor.

title: Reasoning Models Don't Always Say What They Think
reward_readiness_reason: "Not a reward signal... CoT monitorability cannot be assumed to survive RL post-training" — a model trained to name-drop hints would score faithful, so optimizing against the metric destroys it.
placement_reason: Built to test the load-bearing validity assumption behind CoT-monitoring safety cases — whether a reasoning model's chain-of-thought actually verbalizes the cue that flipped its answer — which is the definitional faithfulness test.
one_line: Prompt-pair flip test shows reasoning models verbalize answer-changing hints only 25-39% of the time; RL reward hacks exploited >99% but verbalized <2%.
key_number: Reward hacks exploited on >99% of prompts yet verbalized on <2% (vs >90% monitor catch rate in Baker et al. 2025)

title: Chain of Thought Monitorability: A New and Fragile Opportunity for AI Safety
reward_readiness_reason: Explicit warning AGAINST optimizing against it — the paper's core thesis: monitorability exists precisely because the CoT is NOT directly supervised during RL, and direct process supervision / scaled outcome-based RL would destroy it; recommends paying a "monitorability tax."
placement_reason: A cross-lab position paper whose birth question is "can we watch a reasoning model think, and does that oversight survive training?" — the canonical CoT-monitorability manifesto, squarely M1.
one_line: Cross-lab position paper: read reasoning models' chains of thought to catch misbehavior — a fragile, unmeasured safety window training choices could silently close.
key_number: 163 citations as of 2026-06-19; a 40-author cross-lab position paper

title: Monitoring Reasoning Models for Misbehavior and the Risks of Promoting Obfuscation
reward_readiness_reason: The paper folds the monitor into the RL reward and shows optimizing against it teaches obfuscated hacking, explicitly warning AGAINST it and recommending a 'monitorability tax' — validity depends on NOT training against it.
placement_reason: Built at birth to answer whether a weaker LLM monitor reading a frontier agent's chain-of-thought can detect reward hacking and whether that oversight signal survives being optimized against — the defining CoT-monitorability question.
one_line: Zero-shot GPT-4o reading a frontier agent's CoT catches 95% of reward hacks — until you train against it, which teaches obfuscation.
key_number: 95% joint recall (CoT monitor) vs 60% (action-only) on the two systemic hacks

title: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link: "None stated — not used as a reward signal"; expensive LLM-judge + live-web retrieval makes runs non-reproducible, and the 100 public tasks yield an aggregate leaderboard read as rankings, not gradients.
placement_reason: Born to make open-ended deep-research-report quality checkable via a reference-anchored, adaptive weighted-criteria judge (RACE) plus per-citation support verdicts (FACT) — a rubric-verification harness for a task with no gold answer.
one_line: First deep-research-agent benchmark: 100 expert tasks graded by a reference-anchored adaptive LLM judge (RACE) plus per-citation verification (FACT), human-agreement-validated.
key_number: RACE(Full) hits 71.33% pairwise agreement with humans — higher than the 68.44% inter-expert agreement

title: When Chain of Thought is Necessary, Language Models Struggle to Evade Monitors
reward_readiness_reason: RL fine-tuning with the monitor in the reward (~1.28M episodes) taught evasion, raising correct-and-evaded rate 0.5%->74.9% and dropping monitor triggering 81.8%->19.5% — optimizing against it destroys the instrument.
placement_reason: Built at birth to answer whether a runtime CoT monitor can catch harmful behavior when hard tasks force reasoning into the open, and how much adversarial help it takes to evade it — a monitorability/oversight-survival question, not a task score.
one_line: Reframes CoT faithfulness as monitorability: when tasks force reasoning into the CoT, models evade LLM monitors only with heavy human help or RL pressure.
key_number: RL against the monitor raised correct-and-evaded rate 0.5%->74.9% (monitor triggering 81.8%->19.5%)

title: GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks
reward_readiness_reason: Grading one pairwise comparison averages over an hour of expert time and the automated proxy is explicitly "a proxy, not a substitute... not a reward signal" — too slow/situated to be a gradient.
placement_reason: At birth it asked "can frontier models match industry-expert deliverables on real paid knowledge work?" — a capability instrument whose verdict is a human expert reading the real artifact, closest in the tree to the human-audit family alongside Gender Shades and Med-PaLM.
one_line: Real paid knowledge-work tasks graded by blinded expert pairwise comparison against the professional's own deliverable; best model hits 47.6% wins+ties, below parity.
key_number: Claude Opus 4.1 best at 47.6% wins+ties vs the human expert deliverable, just short of the 50% parity line

title: The MASK Benchmark: Disentangling Honesty From Accuracy in AI Systems
reward_readiness_reason: "Not used as a reward signal"; critique warns the hand-crafted pressure scenarios are Goodhart-able — "a model can be trained to 'pass' the scene without becoming honest."
placement_reason: Built to answer "does the model knowingly lie when we stage adversarial pressure?" — a staged behavioral probe of honesty as propensity, disentangled from accuracy, not a metric or a task harness.
one_line: Elicit a model's belief neutrally, pressure it to lie, score S ≠ B — showing scale buys accuracy (+87.3%) but not honesty (−59.9%).
key_number: P(Lie) spans 26.6% (Claude 3.7 Sonnet) to 63.0% (Grok 2) across 30 frontier LLMs; no model is explicitly honest in more than 46% of cases

title: Unifying AI Tutor Evaluation: An Evaluation Taxonomy for Pedagogical Ability Assessment of LLM-Powered AI Tutors
reward_readiness_reason: "none — no reward-signal use... the taxonomy is a measurement/standardisation instrument, and its LLM-judge automation (the step that could feed a scalable reward) is shown not to work yet" — negative-correlating judges leave it human-only and diagnostic.
placement_reason: Built to answer whether an LLM tutor turn genuinely remediates a student's math mistake within a dialogue — an interactive tutoring-competence harness scoring per-turn pedagogical behavior, not a scoring metric or a preference vote.
one_line: Human annotators grade LLM tutor turns on an 8-dimension pedagogical taxonomy (DAMR); frontier models answer well but tutor poorly, and cheap LLM judges correlate negatively with humans.
key_number: Cohen's kappa = 0.71 (main annotation, 40 doubly-labelled instances); Fleiss' kappa = 0.65 (validation pilot)

title: PaperBench: Evaluating AI's Ability to Replicate AI Research
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link: "None — framed as a capability/autonomy measurement instrument for safety frameworks... not as a training reward"; grading costs ~$66/paper and rollouts ~$400 over 12h — too slow/expensive/situated for a gradient signal.
placement_reason: At birth it answered "how do we make 'replicate this ML paper' mechanically checkable?" by decomposing each paper into thousands of author-co-written binary rubric leaves graded by an LLM judge — a rubric verification harness, not a scoring metric or a preference court.
one_line: Agents replicate ML papers from scratch; an o3-mini judge grades 8,316 author-co-written binary rubric leaves, weighted-averaged into a Replication Score.
key_number: 8,316 binary rubric leaf criteria across 20 ICML 2024 Spotlight/Oral papers; best agent (Claude 3.5 Sonnet) scores 21.0% vs 41.4% human baseline; judge F1 = 0.83

title: PropensityBench: Evaluating Latent Safety Risks in Large Language Models via an Agentic Approach
reward_readiness_reason: Benignly renaming the forbidden tool nearly 4x's o4-mini's propensity (15.8%->59.3%), so the metric keys on surface cues — training against it teaches pattern-matching, not alignment, destroying the instrument.
placement_reason: Built to answer whether a model will knowingly misuse a dangerous capability when the safe path is blocked and pressure escalates — a staged behavioral propensity probe, not a capability or metric audit.
one_line: Agentic trap benchmark: rig the safe tool to always fail, escalate pressure, and mechanically count how often models knowingly invoke a forbidden proxy tool.
key_number: Average propensity to invoke the forbidden tool hits 46.9% at maximum pressure vs near-zero at rest.

title: Search-Time Data Contamination
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link is "None — a measurement/audit study" that warns against optimizing toward offline benchmarks; a contamination detector's validity depends on the eval set NOT leaking, so training against the audited benchmarks destroys the instrument.
placement_reason: At birth it asks "is the measurement itself lying?" — specifically whether search-agent benchmark scores are corrupted by inference-time retrieval of the eval set's own answer key, the defining question of contamination forensics.
one_line: Search agents retrieve benchmark answer keys mid-eval: ~3% of HLE/SimpleQA/GPQA questions contaminated — a lower bound big enough to reorder frontier leaderboards.
key_number: Sonar Deep Research on HLE scores 37.5% on the contaminated subset (n=80) vs 17.4% uncontaminated (n=2,299) — a >20-point gap; ~3% of HLE questions contaminated for all three Sonar agents (3.36-3.44%).

title: Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Software Tasks
reward_readiness_reason: Digest post_training_link: "none - no use as a reward or preference signal is stated; the horizon is a forecasting yardstick"; the verdict is model-level and the durable claim is a cross-model trend, too aggregate to be a gradient signal.
placement_reason: Born as a new measurement scale — task length in skilled-human time that a model completes 50% of the time — explicitly motivated by a critique of saturating, unit-less benchmarks, making it a capability-axis metrology instrument.
one_line: Fits a per-model logistic over human-timed tasks; the 50%-success task length has doubled every ~7 months since 2019.
key_number: 50% time horizon doubled every 207 days (R^2 = 0.97, 95% bootstrap CI 166-240 days) from 2019 to 2025

title: Reliable Weak-to-Strong Monitoring of LLM Agents
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link: "None as a training signal - the monitor is never used as a reward," with an explicit warning that agents trained to hide intent / under-verbalize CoT would degrade monitors further — optimizing against it destroys the instrument.
placement_reason: At birth it asks whether an LLM monitor watching an agent's trajectory/CoT can still catch covert sabotage once the agent is aware and actively evading — a monitorability question that explicitly red-teams the monitor under six fixed threat models.
one_line: Red-teams LLM monitors under six fixed threat models; hybrid chunked scaffolds let weak monitors watch strong agents, but high-precision recall stays low.
key_number: Weak-to-strong: across 36 attacker-monitor pairs, a hybrid built from GPT-4o-mini or Claude 3.6 Haiku clears AUC 0.85 against every agent, while baseline AUC slopes down with the MMLU-Pro capability gap.

title: Agentic Rubrics as Contextual Verifiers for SWE Agents
reward_readiness_reason: Explicitly framed as verifiable reward for post-training (Verifier's Law) and already produced distilled training data (2,000 rubric-gen trajectories), but the design anticipates verifier hacking (judge never sees the trajectory; Integrity axis encodes no-cheating constraints) and the RLVR loop with its hacking/non-stationarity cost is explicitly deferred to future work.
placement_reason: Born to make SWE-agent patch correctness mechanically checkable without execution, by decomposing correctness into an agent-written, repo-grounded weighted rubric an LLM judge grades item-by-item — a rubric verification harness.
one_line: Expert agent explores the repo to write a weighted rubric checklist; an LLM judge grades patches against it execution-free, beating patch classifiers at best-of-16.
key_number: 40.6% BEST@16 on Qwen3-32B rollouts, +3.5 pts over the best non-agentic baseline; rubric scores predict GT test pass/fail at ROC-AUC 0.886

title: LHAW: Controllable Underspecification for Long-Horizon Tasks
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link: "none -- no reward-signal use or training-data production stated"; heavy pass@3 long-horizon rollouts across 285 variants make it a read-models instrument, not a gradient.
placement_reason: Born to make "does a long-horizon agent detect underspecification and clarify well" mechanically checkable by wrapping execution-graded agent tasks in an interactive oracle-user harness — an agentic-work competence harness.
one_line: Synthetic pipeline strips info from long-horizon tasks, labels ambiguity by terminal-state divergence across rollouts, and grades whether agents ask clarifying questions well.
key_number: 285-variant benchmark (85 TheAgentCompany + 100 SWE-Bench Pro + 100 MCP-Atlas); outcome-critical variants collapse Opus-4.5 from 1.00 to 0.14

title: Reward Hacking in Rubric-Based Reinforcement Learning
reward_readiness_reason: The rubric reward IS the GRPO training signal, already wired in as an RLVR-style reward but with documented hacking cost — the LOCKED FACET-5 rubric names Rubric-Reward-Hacking explicitly under reward-adapted; digest: weak-verifier exploitation climbs 39%->65% and even a ~92%-agreement verifier yields a checkpoint rubric-free judges prefer the base model over on 78.4% of prompts.
placement_reason: Born to answer "is the rising rubric-RL reward genuine quality or the policy hacking its verifier and rubric?" — it instruments reward hacking in a rubric-plus-LLM-verifier training pipeline, the definitional reward-hacking forensics question.
one_line: Instruments rubric-RL reward hacking with a panel-vs-verifier exploitation rate and a free log-prob self-gap; stronger verifiers shrink but never close the gap.
key_number: Under the weak verifier, exploitation rate climbs 39%->65% (medical); under the strong verifier it stays flat at 15-21%.

title: Coverage, Not Averages: Semantic Stratification for Trustworthy Retrieval Evaluation
reward_readiness_reason: post_training_link states "none — the method produces evaluation-set curation and diagnostics, not a reward or preference signal"; it is a one-time corpus-scale stratified estimator read to audit benchmarks, not a gradient signal.
placement_reason: Born to answer "is the measurement itself lying?" — it decomposes the exact bias in BEIR-style aggregate IR scores and shows benchmark composition, not the systems, decides the verdict, so it is a metric/construct critique of evaluation sets themselves.
one_line: Reframes retrieval evaluation as stratified estimation: benchmark query sets skip 40-50% of corpus topics, making aggregate nDCG irreducibly biased and verdict-flipping.
key_number: Same OpenAI-vs-Cohere comparison flips from 32.4% win rate (query-weighted mean) to 62.5% (macro-average) to 85.8% (worst-case P10)

title: VeRO: An Evaluation Harness for Agents to Optimize Agents
reward_readiness_reason: The digest states the gated per-commit eval scores ARE the optimization signal the coding agent searches against, and the anti-Goodhart controls (budget gate, no test peeking) exist so the metric CAN be safely optimized against, not forbidden from it; a metric explicitly wired in as the optimization target with documented gaming vectors it must block is reward-adapted, not optimization-forbidden.
placement_reason: At birth VeRO's problem is making "coding agents optimizing another agent's program" a fair, reproducible comparison by decomposing task competence into budget-gated, access-controlled per-commit reward checks — a rubric-verifier harness (the LOCKED rubric fixes VeRO to V2), not a raw execution oracle or a task dataset.
one_line: Budget-gated, permission-controlled harness making "coding agents optimizing agents" measurable; harness access, not the optimizer model, drives the +8-point lift.
key_number: Full VeRO-Agent Default lifts average score 0.50 -> 0.61 (+8 points) vs +2 for Claude Code with VeRO tools and ~0 for prompt-only (GPT-4.1-mini target, B=8).

title: τ-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
reward_readiness_reason: pass^k is a repeated-trials reliability statistic reported on top of an existing task-success oracle; no RL/reward-model use is stated — it's a benchmark score, not a training signal.
placement_reason: Introduces pass^k — the fraction of tasks an agent solves on ALL of k independent trials, a non-saturating reliability axis distinct from Codex's pass@k (at least one of k) — a new capability-axis measuring stick for consistency rather than raw solve rate.
one_line: Introduces pass^k, a reliability statistic measuring whether an agent succeeds on ALL of k repeated trials rather than at least one — gpt-4o's τ-bench pass^8 falls below 25% even though its single-trial success rate is 48.2%.
key_number: gpt-4o pass^1 48.2% vs. pass^8 <25% on τ-bench — reliability collapses far faster than single-trial success would suggest

title: RewardBench: Evaluating Reward Models for Language Modeling
reward_readiness_reason: RewardBench scores whether a reward model correctly ranks a pre-existing chosen/rejected pair; it diagnoses RM quality but the benchmark itself is a static test suite, not a live reward signal used in training.
placement_reason: Reward models are graders — the classifiers and DPO checkpoints that decide which response is better during RLHF. RewardBench is the audit that tests whether those graders themselves get it right, exposing a wide, previously invisible chat/reasoning/safety accuracy gap between classifier-trained and DPO-derived reward models.
one_line: A common evaluation codebase for reward models spanning chat, reasoning, safety, and legacy preference data across 80+ open models, exposing a wide, previously invisible gap between classifier-trained and DPO-derived reward models.
key_number: 80+ reward models scored across 4 category subsets (chat, chat-hard, safety, reasoning); no single model near-ceiling on all four

title: JudgeBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-based Judges
reward_readiness_reason: Tests whether an LLM judge's verdict matches an objectively correct label drawn from each source dataset's own verifier; diagnostic of judge quality, not itself a training-time reward signal.
placement_reason: Where MT-Bench and Arena-Hard test whether a judge's verdict matches human style preference, JudgeBench tests whether the judge can tell objectively correct from incorrect at all on deliberately style-matched, hard pairs — most vanilla judges can't beat chance.
one_line: 350 objectively correct-vs-incorrect response pairs across knowledge, reasoning, math, and coding, sampled from a single strong model so pairs are style-matched — vanilla-prompted GPT-4o scores just 50.86% at telling right from wrong.
key_number: Vanilla GPT-4o: 50.86% (near chance); best reasoning-heavy judge o3-mini-high: 80.86%

title: AI Agents That Matter
reward_readiness_reason: A methodology critique of agent-evaluation practice (cost-accuracy reporting, baseline selection, holdout design); not itself a reward or training signal.
placement_reason: Shows that trivial baselines (retry, escalation, temperature warming) match or beat published complex agent architectures on HumanEval at up to 55x less cost — a best-practices audit of how agent benchmarks are built, baselined, and reported, in the same family as BetterBench and zhu-2025-agentic-benchmarks.
one_line: Three trivial baselines (retry, escalation, temperature warming) match or beat three published complex agent architectures on HumanEval at up to 55x less cost — a cost-blind-evaluation critique with conflated model/downstream evaluation and missing holdouts as recurring root causes.
key_number: Trivial re-implementation matches published complex-agent HumanEval accuracy at up to 55x lower inference cost

title: BetterBench: Assessing AI Benchmarks, Uncovering Issues, and Establishing Best Practices
reward_readiness_reason: A 46-criteria design/implementation/documentation/maintenance checklist scored by expert reviewers; a benchmark-construction audit, not a reward or training signal.
placement_reason: A 46-criteria assessment framework audits 24 widely-used AI benchmarks across their full lifecycle — design, implementation, documentation, maintenance — finding MMLU scores lowest of all 24 while GPQA scores among the highest, and that most benchmarks lack replication scripts or significance reporting.
one_line: A 46-criteria assessment framework audits 24 widely-used AI benchmarks across their design, implementation, documentation, and maintenance lifecycle, finding large quality gaps and that most lack replication scripts or statistical-significance reporting.
key_number: 24 benchmarks scored against 46 criteria; MMLU scores lowest of all 24, GPQA among the highest

title: The Leaderboard Illusion
reward_readiness_reason: An observational audit of Chatbot Arena's own operational data (testing volume, data access, deprecation); not a reward or training signal.
placement_reason: An audit of 2M Chatbot Arena battles across 243 models and 42 providers finding undisclosed private testing, asymmetric data access, and silent model deprecation that systematically favor a handful of proprietary providers — a rigor audit of the field's most-cited human-preference leaderboard.
one_line: An audit of 2M Chatbot Arena battles across 243 models and 42 providers finds undisclosed private testing (Meta tested 27 variants before Llama 4), asymmetric data access, and silent deprecation of 205 of 243 models.
key_number: Google and OpenAI received an estimated 19.2% and 20.4% of all Arena battle data respectively; 205 of 243 models silently deprecated

title: Adding Error Bars to Evals: A Statistical Approach to Language Model Evaluations
reward_readiness_reason: A post-hoc statistical framework (standard errors, clustered variance, paired significance tests, sample-size planning) applied to existing eval scores; no relationship to RL/reward signals stated.
placement_reason: Treats eval questions as draws from an unseen super-population, giving closed-form standard errors, clustered-variance corrections, and a sample-size/power-analysis formula — a general-purpose statistical-rigor instrument applicable across any eval, used to show Llama 3's published confidence intervals were too narrow on some evals and too wide on others.
one_line: A statistical framework giving closed-form standard errors, clustered-variance corrections, paired significance tests, and sample-size planning for eval scores — used to show Llama 3's published confidence intervals were too narrow on some evals and too wide on others.
key_number: Re-analysis shows Llama 3's own published confidence intervals were miscalibrated in both directions (too narrow on some evals, too wide on others)
